current_revision.content:
Definition

The Experience Engine is a formal specification for systems that process raw experience into interpretation through perspectival context. It specifies what an honest record requires — one that preserves the distinction between what happened, how it connects, and what it means.

It is defined as a tuple:

𝓔 = (G, S, M, π, γ, σ)

where:

  • G is the Given-Log: what happened.

  • S is the Structure-Lattice: how what happened is connected.

  • M is the Meant-Graph: what it means.

  • π is the provenance function: maps interpretations to their grounding in raw experience.

  • γ is the availability function: maps positions in the Structure-Lattice to accessible entries and interpretations.

  • σ is the supersession function: maps position-interpretation pairs to interpretations they may override.

The three data structures correspond to the three domains of the capacity ground:

Component

Domain

Question

Given-Log (G)

Existence

Whether things are

Structure-Lattice (S)

Structure

How things connect

Meant-Graph (M)

Significance

What things mean

The three functions collectively constitute the Horizon — the act of looking. The Horizon is not a data structure. It is a projection: an INS of a query run against G, S, and M from a position within S. Every observation instantiates a projection. There is no unmediated access.

The Given-Log (G)

Specification

An append-only sequence of raw experience records. No record is ever edited, overwritten, or deleted. The log grows monotonically. ### Epistemic status: phenomena, not noumena Every entry in the Given-Log is a **phenomenon** in the Kantian sense: an appearance of something to an observer operating within a particular frame, not the thing-in-itself. The log does not record reality as it is independent of observation. It records reality as it *appeared* — to a specific agent, in a specific mode, within a specific context, at a specific time. This is why the record fields are not administrative metadata. They are the epistemic constitution of the entry: - **Agent** — whose observation this is. No observation is observer-free. - **Mode of givenness** — how it was encountered (perceived, reported, measured, received). The mode shapes what can appear. - **Context envelope** — the frame within which the appearance occurred. The same event appears differently across frames. An entry stripped of these fields would not be a leaner record. It would be a different kind of claim — a claim about the noumenon, which the system has no access to and makes no claims about. The Given-Log's append-only immutability is not a truth-preservation guarantee. What cannot be erased is the *fact that this observation occurred, from this position, in this mode*. Whether the observation reflects observer-independent reality is not asserted, cannot be stored, and is not the system's concern. That adjudication belongs to the Meant-Graph. This is the structural basis of Rule 4 (Anti-Omniscience Axiom): there is no God's-eye view because the Given-Log only records phenomena, and phenomena are always frame-specific. **Truth in EO is not a value the Given-Log holds. It is a limit the system approaches.** As observations accumulate from multiple positions and frames are refined through DEF and REC, the Meant-Graph converges asymptotically toward what those observations are about. The deepest coordinate in EO's framework — 2^√2, the Pattern position of the Significance triad — is transcendental by the Gelfond-Schneider theorem: unreachable by any finite algebraic process, approachable only in the limit. That is the formal address of truth in EO. ### Epistemic status: phenomena, not noumena Every entry in the Given-Log is a **phenomenon** in the Kantian sense: an appearance of something to an observer operating within a particular frame, not the thing-in-itself. The log does not record reality as it is independent of observation. It records reality as it *appeared* — to a specific agent, in a specific mode, within a specific context, at a specific time. This is why the record fields are not administrative metadata. They are the epistemic constitution of the entry: - **Agent** — whose observation this is. No observation is observer-free. - **Mode of givenness** — how it was encountered (perceived, reported, measured, received). The mode shapes what can appear. - **Context envelope** — the frame within which the appearance occurred. The same event appears differently across frames. An entry stripped of these fields would not be a leaner record. It would be a different kind of claim — a claim about the noumenon, which the system has no access to and makes no claims about. The Given-Log's append-only immutability is not a truth-preservation guarantee. What cannot be erased is the *fact that this observation occurred, from this position, in this mode*. Whether the observation reflects observer-independent reality is not asserted, cannot be stored, and is not the system's concern. That adjudication belongs to the Meant-Graph. This is the structural basis of Rule 4 (Anti-Omniscience Axiom): there is no God's-eye view because the Given-Log only records phenomena, and phenomena are always frame-specific. **Truth in EO is not a value the Given-Log holds. It is a limit the system approaches.** As observations accumulate from multiple positions and frames are refined through DEF and REC, the Meant-Graph converges asymptotically toward what those observations are about. The deepest coordinate in EO's framework — 2^√2, the Pattern position of the Significance triad — is transcendental by the Gelfond-Schneider theorem: unreachable by any finite algebraic process, approachable only in the limit. That is the formal address of truth in EO.

Each record must capture four things. These requirements — not a flat field list — generate the record structure.

1. That it occurred. When, by whom, and with a unique reference. These apply to all nine cell types, though for NUL rows "who recognized the absence" is weaker than "who instantiated the entity" — absence is discovered, not authored.

2. What kind of event it is. The operator address: Mode × Object on the Existence face. This is the cell type. It determines the structure of everything else. A SEN × Ground entry (something ambient became distinguishable) is not the same shape as an INS × Figure entry (a specific entity crossed into the particular). The cell type is not a tag on a uniform record — it is the record type.

3. What was registered. The content of the distinction. Ground-row content is ambient — no stable referent. Figure-row content has an identity. Pattern-row content has a structure. Forcing all three into a single "phenomenal content" field is the schema-before-instance error. NUL rows have no content in this sense — they have an absence condition instead.

4. The absence condition. Applicable only to NUL rows. The three-state marker (cleared / unknown / never-set) is the content of an absence record. See Three NUL States below.

These four requirements generate the record's parameters. The table below is a requirements matrix: one record structure, with cell determining which parameters are required and what shape they take.

Parameter

Required for

Shape

Timestamp

All entries

When the event occurred

Agent / recognizer

All entries

Who registered it — full agent for SEN and INS; recognizer only (weak) for NUL, since absence is discovered not authored

ID

SEN × Figure; all INS rows

Assigned at the moment a thing becomes referenceable; NUL rows and SEN × Ground carry no ID — absence and ambient distinguishability do not yet instantiate a particular

Prior reference

NUL × Figure; NUL × Pattern

Points back to what was present before; required to specify what is now absent

Kind

All entries

The cell address — Mode × Object on the Existence face; determines the shape of everything else

Content

SEN and INS rows only

Shape varies by Object axis: Ground rows — ambient, no stable referent; Figure rows — entity, has identity; Pattern rows — pattern, spans multiple figures

Absence state

NUL rows only

Cleared / unknown / never-set; this is the content of an absence record — NUL rows carry no content parameter

INS × Figure is the only cell that requires all parameters except absence state, which is why prior specifications silently universalized from it.

Mathematical Character

A time-ordered sequence of partial projections. Each entry is a field — a face-projection of a phenomenon onto the Existence domain — not a complete snapshot of it. The phenomenon being recorded is never fully contained in any single entry; it exists across the interleave of successive entries, the way an interlaced signal only resolves in the combine of two fields.

This has a direct consequence for entity recognition: a single Given-Log entry cannot ground a claim about entity type (emanon, protogon, holon). Entity type is a trajectory signature — a pattern recognized over a window of entries, not a property readable from any one point. A holon is a trajectory whose window reveals periodicity. An emanon is a trajectory that resists periodicity across windows of any tested length. A protogon is a trajectory with directional momentum but no established period. The window specification — grain, length, position in the Structure-Lattice — is part of any well-formed entity-type claim.

Single-entry reads are face-projections. Entity-type claims require a window.

Three NUL States

The Given-Log distinguishes three structurally distinct kinds of absence. These are not subtypes of a single NULL category. They carry different information about the sequence of prior frames and have different downstream effects on the Meant-Graph and the Structure-Lattice.

State

Meaning

Implied sequence

Operator

Cleared

Was present; now absent

Something was registered here and has been superseded

NUL acting on a prior INS

Unknown

Applicable but unregistered

The slot exists in the schema; no observation has been made

SEN without INS

Never-set

No history for this slot

This position was not part of any prior projection

Pre-SEN

Collapsing these three states into a single absence marker — as SQL's NULL does — destroys the information that makes a sequence of entries readable as motion. Codd recognized two of these in 1990 (A-mark for missing-but-applicable, I-mark for missing-but-inapplicable) and proposed encoding them explicitly. The database industry rejected both and retained a single NULL. The Given-Log encodes all three. See The Projective Constraint for the geometric argument for why this matters.

Triad Correspondence

The Existence triad (NUL, SEN, INS) governs operations on the Given-Log:

  • NUL: A record registers absence — a measurement returned no result, an expected event did not occur. The Given-Log holds absence as a positive entry, with the three-state marker specifying which kind.

  • SEN: A record registers a distinction — something was noticed. Every entry in the Given-Log is itself a SEN event.

  • INS: A record instantiates a specific experience — brings it into the log as a concrete datum.

The Structure-Lattice (S)

Specification

A partially ordered set of relationships between Given-Log entries. Encodes boundaries, connections, and irreducible composites. Simultaneously defines the space of possible perspectives — where an observer can stand determines what they can see, and where they can stand is determined by the structure.

Mathematical Character

A lattice. Richer than a graph (which encodes only pairwise connections). The Structure-Lattice holds:

  • Partitions (SEG): boundaries that divide entries into regions.

  • Joins (CON): connections across boundaries.

  • Composites (SYN): irreducible wholes — higher-order relationships not decomposable into pairwise connections.

The partial ordering encodes refinement: a more specific position in the lattice sees a subset of what a more general position sees.

The Structure-Lattice also defines the space of valid windows for Given-Log reads. A window is a bounded region of the lattice; its grain (SEG), connectivity (CON), and composite structure (SYN) determine what trajectory signatures are recoverable from it.

Triad Correspondence

The Structure triad (SEG, CON, SYN) governs the lattice:

  • SEG: Draws boundaries. Partitions the Given-Log into regions.

  • CON: Connects across boundaries. Creates joins between regions.

  • SYN: Produces irreducible composites. Creates nodes in the lattice whose content exceeds the union of their parts.

The Horizon as Position in the Structure-Lattice

The structure of relationships between entries is the space of possible perspectives. These are not two things. Where you can stand to observe is determined by how the system is connected, the way that the architecture of a building determines the possible viewpoints within it.

The Horizon-Lattice of the original specification (v1) was the Structure-Lattice under a different name. The v1 specification assigned it to a dedicated component, creating the appearance of a fourth element. The revision recognizes that Structure and Horizon are the same object: the lattice that encodes both how entries relate and where observers can stand.

The three functions (π, γ, σ) are operations within this lattice:

  • γ (availability): From this position, what is visible? Determined by the lattice topology.

  • π (provenance): From this interpretation, what is the chain back to raw experience? A path through the lattice.

  • σ (supersession): From this position, which interpretations override which? Determined by lattice ordering and available evidence.

The Meant-Graph (M)

Specification

A mutable space of interpretations. Every interpretation carries:

  • A link to the Given-Log entries it is grounded in (via π)

  • A position in the Structure-Lattice from which it was produced

  • A window specification — the grain and bounds of the Given-Log read that produced it

  • Content (what the interpretation asserts)

  • Supersession relationships to other interpretations

Unlike the Given-Log, the Meant-Graph can be restructured. Interpretations can be added, superseded, recontextualized, and retired. The provenance chain must remain intact for audit.

Mathematical Character

Three-dimensional. Each interpretation occupies a position in the capacity ground — a coordinate specifying Mode (how it was derived), Domain (what domain it addresses), and Object (what it targets). The Meant-Graph is a populated capacity ground: the 27-cell space with interpretations at specific coordinates.

If DEF is operative, an interpretation may occupy a superposition of positions — holding multiple coordinates simultaneously without resolution.

Entity-type attributions (emanon, protogon, holon) live in the Meant-Graph, not the Given-Log. They are interpretations grounded in a window of raw experience, not properties of individual records.

Triad Correspondence

The Significance triad (EVA, DEF, REC) governs the Meant-Graph:

  • EVA: An interpretation changes. A prior reading is superseded by a new one. Value shifts within frame.

  • DEF: Two or more interpretations coexist without resolution. The Meant-Graph holds contradictory readings simultaneously.

  • REC: The interpretive frame itself is revised. Not a new interpretation within the existing frame, but a change to what counts as a legitimate interpretation.

The Nine Rules

Nine rules govern the system, organized in three triads mirroring the operator structure.

Given-Conformant (Experiential Integrity)

Rule 1 — Distinction. Given and Meant are exhaustive and exclusive categories. Every record is one or the other, never both, never neither. Violation: Categorical Confusion.

Rule 2 — Impenetrability. Given derives only from Given. No interpretation can fabricate raw experience. Violation: Confabulation.

Rule 3 — Ineliminability (The Anti-Gaslighting Axiom). Raw experience persists through all operations. The Given-Log is append-only. The past actually happened. Violation: Gaslighting.

Structure-Conformant (Perspectival Coherence)

Rule 4 — Perspectivality (The Anti-Omniscience Axiom). There is no God's-eye view. All availability is mediated by position in the Structure-Lattice. Violation: Context Collapse.

Rule 5 — Restrictivity. Refinement only restricts availability. A more specific position sees a subset, never a superset. Violation: Foreclosure Violation.

Rule 6 — Coherence. Availability is consistent across overlapping positions. Where two positions both see a Given-Log entry, they agree on its existence. Violation: Perspectival Fracture.

Meant-Conformant (Interpretive Accountability)

Rule 7 — Groundedness. Every interpretation traces to raw experience through the provenance function. No free-floating interpretations. Violation: Ungrounded Assertion.

Rule 8 — Determinacy. Meaning is what survives transformation under all legitimate operations — not essence, but equivalence class. Violation: Semantic Drift.

Rule 9 — Defeasibility. No interpretation is globally immune to supersession. ¬∃ m* immune to supersession. Violation: Dogmatism.

Relationship to Prior Art

What Is Standard

Codd's relational model (1970). Views, parameterized queries, query containment, and the ANSI/SPARC three-level architecture (external schema / conceptual schema / internal schema) provide the structural precedent. The internal schema corresponds to the Given-Log, the conceptual schema to the Structure-Lattice, and the external schema to the Horizon (the projection). Codd had this in the 1970s.

Event sourcing (Fowler, Young). Append-only logs, replay-to-state derivation, immutable events. The Given-Log inherits this architecture directly.

Git. Immutable commits (Given-Log), branchable state (Meant-Graph), checked-out views (Horizon). The structural parallel is close.

What Is Not Standard

Rules 1–3. The enforced separation between raw experience and interpretation, the prohibition on fabrication of experience by interpretation, and the append-only commitment have no equivalent in the relational model. SQL permits UPDATE and DELETE on historical records. SQL permits a row representing an interpretation to be structurally indistinguishable from a row representing a measurement. The relational model does not enforce the Given/Meant distinction.

The three NUL states. Event sourcing systems distinguish domain-named events but do not formally distinguish cleared, unknown, and never-set absence. The Given-Log requires all three as a formal invariant, not a convention.

The window specification on interpretations. Standard event sourcing derives current state by replaying the full log. The Experience Engine's Meant-Graph entries carry an explicit window specification — grain, bounds, lattice position — making the derivation auditable and the trajectory signature recoverable. Entity-type claims are grounded interpretations with window provenance, not implicit state.

Rule 4 as ontological commitment. Codd's views are engineering conveniences — different users need different slices of the same underlying truth. The Experience Organ's perspectivality is an epistemological claim: there is no underlying truth independent of perspective. The projection is fundamental, not convenient.

Rule 9 as structural constraint. Defeasibility is encoded as a system invariant (¬∃ m* immune to supersession), not stated as a methodological norm. No existing database system enforces this.

The closed operator algebra. Where event sourcing uses an open vocabulary of domain-named events, EO replaces this with a closed algebra of nine operators, making events portable across domains.

Dimensional Summary

Component

Domain

Mathematical Object

Note

Given-Log

Existence

Time-ordered sequence of partial projections

Each entry is a field; phenomena exist across windows

Structure-Lattice

Structure

Lattice (partially ordered, with composites)

Defines both relations and the space of valid windows

Meant-Graph

Significance

Populated capacity ground (27-cell space)

Interpretations carry window provenance
